original_abstract: This review highlights in situ UV-vis-NIR range absorption spectroscopy in catalysis. A variety of experimental techniques identifying reaction mechanisms, kinetics, and structural properties are discussed. Stopped flow techniques, use of laser pulses, and use of experimental perturbations are demonstrated for in situ studies of enzymatic, homogeneous, heterogeneous, and photocatalysis. They access different time scales and are applicable to different reaction systems and catalyst types. In photocatalysis, femto- and nanosecond resolved measurements through transient absorption are discussed for tracking excited states. UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopies for structural characterization are demonstrated especially for Cu and Fe exchanged zeolites and metalloenzymes. This requires combining different spectroscopies. Combining magnetic circular dichroism and resonance Raman spectroscopy is especially powerful. A multitude of phenomena can be tracked on transition metal catalysts on various supports, including changes in oxidation state, adsorptions, reactions, support interactions, surface plasmon resonances, and band gaps. Measurements of oxidation states, oxygen vacancies, and band gaps are shown on heterogeneous catalysts, especially for electrocatalysis. UV-vis-NIR absorption is burdened by broad absorption bands. Advanced analysis techniques enable the tracking of coking reactions on acid zeolites despite convoluted spectra. The value of UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy to catalyst characterization and mechanistic investigation is clear but could be expanded.
humanized_abstract: The paper reviews the use of in situ UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy in catalysis research along with other techniques such as identification of catalytic mechanisms and rates of catalysts as well as structural and morphological properties of catalysts. This includes stopped flow experiments as well as laser pulse experiments with additional perturbations such as temperature, pressure, optical stirring and catalytic reactions over enzymatic, heterogeneous and homogeneous as well as photocatalysts. They work on various time scales for different catalytic reactions and catalysts. Femto- and nanosecond resolved data for transient absorption spectroscopy measurements is used to trace the excited states in photocatalysis. UV-vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy for the structural characterization of catalysts is demonstrated by using Cu and Fe exchanged zeolites and also on enzymes. This requires a combination of spectroscopies, like in combining MCD and Resonance Raman spectroscopy. A large number of phenomena have been traced by applying UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy on transition metal catalysts on various supports such as: changes in the oxidation state of the active component, adsorption reactions, support-metal interaction, surface plasmon resonance, band gaps etc. Quantitative measurements of oxidation states, oxygen vacancies and band gaps have also been shown on heterogeneous catalysts and especially on electrocatalysts. Even though UV-vis-NIR is burdened by the fact that it measures broad absorption bands, it can be developed by using highly developed analysis techniques in order to identify coking reactions on acid zeolites. In conclusion, even if the UV-vis-NIR spectroscopy is very useful for the characterization of catalysts as well as their mechanism, it could be extended to other applications.
